title: Backup coverage gap — regulated workloads with no successful backup in the protection window
exclusions: Does NOT measure restore correctness (UC-22.45.1) or integrity protection of the backup itself (UC-22.45.2). Focused on coverage: every workload the CMDB / regulated-asset inventory marks in-scope must have at least one successful backup-job completion within its tier's protection window. Does not cover ad-hoc workloads (laptops, BYOD) — those are out of scope for the regulated-workload classes considered here.
evidence: Daily saved search `UC-22.45.3` emits one row per regulated workload that has no successful backup-job event within its protection window, plus a positive-coverage attestation table for the steering pack. Output bundle: (a) ServiceNow GRC `BCP-COVERAGE-GAP` ticket per finding, (b) signed CSV in `index=audit_evidence` (7y retention), (c) weekly executive PDF showing coverage percentage by regulated class (PCI / HIPAA / GDPR / SOX), (d) RFC3161 countersignature.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CP-9 mandates backup of system-level AND user-level information at an organisation-defined frequency. The control fails as soon as a single in-scope system has no recent backup. This UC is the single primary evidence for that completeness claim — full assurance.
compliance.controlObjective: Continuously evidences CP-9 coverage by joining the regulated-workload inventory to backup-job history and producing both a finding feed (uncovered workloads) and a positive attestation (covered workloads), making coverage measurable not assertable.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.45.3`. Daily CSV digest in `index=audit_evidence` lists (a) all uncovered workloads as findings AND (b) the positive attestation: coverage % by regulated class. RFC3161 countersignature. 7-year retention.

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.12(1) requires comprehensive backup policies covering ICT systems supporting critical and important functions. Coverage is the threshold test; if any in-scope system is missing, the policy is not enforced. This UC enforces the threshold test, but doesn't alone evidence the policy text or the recovery testing — partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Demonstrates to the DORA supervisor that backup policies are enforced uniformly across all ICT systems supporting critical and important functions — coverage is measured nightly and gaps are visible same-day.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.45.3` daily digest in `audit_evidence` (5+ year retention). Pairs with UC-22.45.1 (testing) for joint Art.12 evidence.

compliance.assurance_rationale: ITGC backup-and-restore controls are foundational to SOX IT general controls. External auditors test coverage by sampling: 'show me the backup proof for these N financial-system hosts in this period'. A continuous coverage attestation passes that test the first time, every time.
compliance.controlObjective: Replaces sampling-based audit testing of SOX-relevant backup coverage with a continuous attestation that every financial system has a recent successful backup, fully traceable to the configuration item in the CMDB.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.45.3` daily attestation filtered to `regulated_class=SOX`. Signed PDF in `audit_evidence` aligned with the SOX 7-year retention requirement.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 9.4.3 requires backups of cardholder-data systems be stored securely; the precondition is that the backup happened at all. This UC enforces the precondition for any workload tagged `regulated_class=PCI`. Storage security is evidenced by UC-22.45.2 (immutability) and UC-22.45.4 (TLS) so partial here.
compliance.controlObjective: Catches PCI cardholder-data systems that silently lost their backup coverage — the most common cause of an avoidable QSA finding.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.45.3` filtered to `regulated_class=PCI`. 1-year QSA-aligned retention guaranteed by the broader 7-year `audit_evidence` index.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Insert a row into `regulated_workloads.csv` with `workload_id=SAP-PRD-NEW`, `regulated_class=SOX`, `tier=1`, `protection_window_hours=24` and intentionally do NOT ingest any successful backup-job events for that workload over the next 25 hours. After the next scheduled run the search must emit a row: `workload_id=SAP-PRD-NEW`, `finding=no_backup_in_window`, `hours_since_last_success` ≥ 25, `protection_window_hours=24`, `regulated_class=SOX`. Conversely, mark a workload as `regulated_class=PCI` in the lookup but only have FAILED backup events for it — the search must still flag it (failed events do not count as protection).
controlTest.negativeScenario: A workload with at least one successful backup-job event (`status=success`) within its `protection_window_hours` must NOT appear. A workload whose `decommissioned_at` field in the CMDB is non-null and ≤ now() must NOT appear (decommissioned workloads are out of scope; their absence from backups is correct). A workload whose `regulated_class` lookup value is empty must NOT appear (it is not regulated) — but it should be counted in a sister-search alert for inventory completeness.

dataSources:
Two-stream join.
Stream A (backup history): `index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status` ingested via Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601), Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876, AWS Backup job CloudWatch events), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110, Azure Recovery Services Vault diagnostics), plus HEC for Commvault, NetBackup, Rubrik, Cohesity, PowerProtect. Required fields: `workload_id` (normalised from `host`/`vm_name`/`client_name`/`backup_job_name`/`resource_id`), `status` (success|failed|warning|partial), `completed_at`, `duration_sec`, `bytes_protected`.
Stream B (regulated-workload register): `regulated_workloads.csv` (or KV Store `regulated_workloads`) — the canonical inventory. Required columns: `workload_id`, `regulated_class` (PCI/HIPAA/GDPR/SOX), `tier`, `protection_window_hours`, `cmdb_ci`, `business_owner`, `decommissioned_at` (nullable). Sourced from the CMDB (ServiceNow, BMC Helix, Device42, Lansweeper) refreshed daily. Required to be version-controlled in git.
Lookup: `dr_tiers.csv` (same as UC-22.45.1) to enrich with `tier_name` for the steering pack.
Evidence archive: `index=audit_evidence` (7y).
app: Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601), Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) for the CMDB lookup pipeline. Optional: Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841) for service-tree linkage from workload → business service.
spl:
| inputlookup regulated_workloads.csv
| where isnull(decommissioned_at) OR strptime(decommissioned_at,"%Y-%m-%d") > now()
| eval protection_window_hours=coalesce(protection_window_hours, case(tier=="1",24, tier=="2",72, tier=="3",168, true(),168))
| eval window_seconds = protection_window_hours * 3600
| eval earliest_protected = now() - window_seconds
| join type=left workload_id [
    search index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status earliest=-14d
    | eval workload_id=coalesce(host, vm_name, client_name, resource_id, backup_job_name)
    | eval job_status=lower(coalesce(status, job_result, state))
    | where job_status=="success"
    | stats max(_time) as last_success_at, count as success_count_14d by workload_id
  ]
| eval has_successful_backup_in_window = if(isnotnull(last_success_at) AND last_success_at >= earliest_protected, 1, 0)
| eval hours_since_last_success = if(isnotnull(last_success_at), round((now()-last_success_at)/3600,1), null())
| where has_successful_backup_in_window == 0
| eval finding = if(isnull(last_success_at), "no_backup_record_found", "no_backup_in_window")
| lookup dr_tiers.csv tier OUTPUT tier_name
| eval last_success_at_iso = if(isnotnull(last_success_at), strftime(last_success_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z"), "never")
| table regulated_class workload_id tier tier_name finding hours_since_last_success protection_window_hours last_success_at_iso cmdb_ci business_owner
| sort 0 regulated_class tier - hours_since_last_success
description: Joins the regulated-workload inventory (from the CMDB) to the actual backup-job history and emits any workload that has not had a successful backup within its tier-defined protection window. The two findings — `no_backup_record_found` (we have never seen a backup) and `no_backup_in_window` (last success too old) — are reported separately because they have different operational causes and runbooks.
value: Coverage gaps are by a margin the single most common backup-related finding at SOX, SOC 2, PCI and DORA audits, because the gap is silent: the backup admin sees green jobs, the auditor sees a missing system. Continuous CMDB-to-backup reconciliation eliminates the surprise finding and shifts coverage from 'we hope so' to a defended number with a named workload owner against every gap.
implementation: (1) Stand up `regulated_workloads.csv` from the CMDB via the ServiceNow TA (or equivalent) — this is the entire battle: an authoritative, fresh asset list. Refresh nightly; version in git. (2) Ingest `backup:job_status` from every backup engine; use existing Veeam TA path for Veeam, HEC for the rest. (3) Schedule this search nightly at 04:00 site-local looking back 14 days. (4) Route findings to ServiceNow GRC `BCP-COVERAGE-GAP` and email the steering pack PDF every Monday. (5) Tee the positive-coverage attestation to `audit_evidence`.
visualization: Dashboard `UC-22.45.3 — Backup Coverage`: (1) Single-value tiles per regulated class (PCI/HIPAA/GDPR/SOX) — '% of workloads covered in window' (red if <100% on PCI/HIPAA/SOX, amber if <99.5% on GDPR). (2) Sortable finding table from the SPL with row drilldown to `index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status workload_id=$row.workload_id$ earliest=-30d`. (3) Heatmap of `regulated_class × week` over 13 weeks showing the gap count — trend should be flat or descending. (4) Per-business-owner table with column 'open gaps' sorted descending — the steering pack uses this row. (5) Top-N of `cmdb_ci` types contributing the most gaps over 30 days; commonly surfaces a class of workload (e.g. a new container platform) that's not in the backup scope by design — points to a controls-design problem rather than a control-execution problem.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601)
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) — CMDB integration
AWS Backup — CloudWatch Events for backup jobs
Azure Backup — Monitor Recovery Services vault jobs
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — CP-9 System Backup
PCI DSS v4.0 — Requirement 9.4 Securely store media (including backups)

knownFalsePositives:
**(1) Workloads with no backup AGENT installed by design.** Some workloads are protected via snapshot at the storage/hypervisor layer and never emit `backup:job_status` events — the agent is absent because the design uses array-based replication. These appear as `finding=no_backup_record_found`. Mitigate by ingesting the storage-array snapshot inventory (Pure / NetApp / Dell PowerStore) as a second backup-history stream, OR by tagging the workload `protection_method=array_snapshot` in `regulated_workloads.csv` and adding `| where protection_method!="array_snapshot"` to the SPL.

**(2) Newly-onboarded workloads inside the protection window of their bootstrap.** A workload provisioned 6 hours ago, against a tier-1 24-hour SLO, hasn't completed its first backup yet — but it's not a gap, it's a normal bootstrap. Add a `provisioned_at` column to `regulated_workloads.csv` and append `| where now() - strptime(provisioned_at,"%Y-%m-%d") > 2*window_seconds` to suppress the first protection-window-multiplied window.

**(3) Hypervisor-replicated DR pairs counted twice.** A workload in active/passive DR has two CMDB entries (`SAP-PRD-01-PRIMARY` and `SAP-PRD-01-DR`), but the backup engine only protects the primary while the secondary inherits via replication. Maintain a `replication_pairs.csv` keyed on the DR copy's `workload_id` pointing to its primary, and add `| lookup replication_pairs.csv workload_id OUTPUT primary_workload_id | eval workload_id=coalesce(primary_workload_id, workload_id)` BEFORE the join. The downstream evidence still lists both, but the backup-history check happens against the primary.

**(4) Backup-policy renames that orphan history.** When a backup admin renames a job, the new name appears in `backup:job_status` events but the old name remains in `regulated_workloads.csv` until the CMDB refresh runs. For up to one day after a rename, the workload appears as a gap. Avoid by joining on a stable CI identifier (`cmdb_ci`) rather than the friendly name. The SPL already prefers `resource_id` (cloud) and `host` (canonical FQDN) over `backup_job_name`.

**(5) Time-zone clock skew on the CMDB export.** A nightly CMDB dump that arrives at 06:00 UTC but lists the workloads with `provisioned_at` in PST will appear to be 8 hours newer than reality. The previous suppression depends on `provisioned_at`; if your CMDB export is inconsistent on timezone, this UC will false-positive on the FP suppression. Normalise `provisioned_at` to ISO-8601 with timezone at export time.

**(6) Suppression mechanism for documented exceptions.** Maintain `coverage_exception_register` KV Store with (`workload_id`, `granted_by`, `valid_from`, `valid_until`, `reason`, `compensating_control`). The SPL appends `| lookup coverage_exception_register workload_id OUTPUT valid_until reason | where isnull(valid_until) OR valid_until < now()`. Every exception must be reviewable by the steering committee and have a valid_until — open-ended exceptions are themselves a finding.
equipmentModels: Cohesity Helios DataProtect — Run-state REST API v2
Commvault Command Center 11.x — Job History REST API
Dell PowerProtect Data Manager — Activities REST API
Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876, AWS Backup job CloudWatch events)
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110, Azure Recovery Services Vault diagnostics)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) for CMDB regulated-asset list
Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601)
Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841) for business-service overlay
Veritas NetBackup OpsCenter 10.x — Job Search REST API

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional; provides workload → business-service mapping for the steering pack

detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

- **Splunk:** Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (Victoria). This UC's join across 14 days of backup events is bursty — schedule it during off-hours and consider running on a dedicated search head.
- **Indexes:** `backup` (raw backup-job events, 90d retention), `audit_evidence` (7y, signed). The CMDB lookup `regulated_workloads.csv` lives in the search-tier app, not in an index.
- **Add-ons:** Veeam (5601), AWS (1876) with the `aws_inputs` `cloudwatch_logs` input configured for the AWS Backup log group, Microsoft Cloud Services (3110) with Recovery Services Vault diagnostic settings forwarded, ServiceNow (1928) for the daily CMDB pull.
- **Critical lookup:** `regulated_workloads.csv`. This is the entire battle for this UC — an authoritative, fresh, complete inventory of what is in regulatory scope. Required columns:
  - `workload_id` (key) — canonical name: hostname/FQDN for VMs, AWS resource ARN suffix for AWS, Azure resource ID short form for Azure, container service name for k8s.
  - `regulated_class` — one of `PCI`, `HIPAA`, `GDPR`, `SOX`, `MULTI` (use `MULTI` if a workload sits in more than one regime and add a parallel column `additional_classes` for tooling that needs the list).
  - `tier` — 1/2/3 (matches `dr_tiers.csv` semantics).
  - `protection_window_hours` — explicit. If absent, the SPL falls back to tier defaults but the explicit value is preferred for audit clarity.
  - `cmdb_ci` — the canonical CMDB identifier (e.g. ServiceNow `sys_id` or BMC Helix CI ID). Lets auditors pivot to the master record.
  - `business_owner` — accountable owner name + email.
  - `provisioned_at` — ISO-8601 with timezone.
  - `decommissioned_at` — nullable ISO-8601 with timezone. Past-dated rows are filtered out at the start of the search.
  - `protection_method` — optional; one of `agent`, `array_snapshot`, `hypervisor_snapshot`, `database_native`. Allows excluding workloads protected outside the backup engine's view.
- **CMDB freshness:** the lookup MUST be refreshed within 24 hours; staler-than-24h causes false negatives (a decommissioned workload still in the lookup) and false positives (a new workload not yet in the lookup, flagged as `no_backup_record_found`). Run a sister-alert: `| inputlookup regulated_workloads.csv | stats latest(_lookup_mtime) as last | eval age_h=round((now()-last)/3600,1) | where age_h > 26`.
- **RBAC:** users running the search need `srchIndexesAllowed = backup,aws,azure,audit_evidence` and access to the lookup. The CMDB feed runs as a service account `svc_cmdb_export`.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Three data streams must be ingested before this UC produces correct output. All three are independent and each adds coverage breadth.

### 1a — On-prem backup engines

For Veeam, the existing Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication ships `backup:job_status` from `GET /api/jobs/states`. Configure on the heavy forwarder:
```ini
[veeam_backup_status://job_status]
veeam_em_host = veeam-em.example.com
veeam_em_port = 9398
veeam_em_user = svc_splunk
resource = /api/jobs/states
interval = 600
index = backup
sourcetype = backup:job_status
```

For Commvault, NetBackup, Rubrik, Cohesity, PowerProtect — re-use the HEC pollers introduced by UC-22.45.1 but post to `sourcetype=backup:job_status` (the canonical pass/fail stream), not `backup:restore_test`. Canonical schema:
```json
{
  "event": "backup_job_completed",
  "job_id": "<vendor-job-id>",
  "workload_id": "SAP-PRD-01",
  "status": "success|failed|partial|warning",
  "started_at": "2026-05-13T01:00:00+00:00",
  "completed_at": "2026-05-13T01:11:38+00:00",
  "duration_sec": 698,
  "bytes_protected": 482194304
}
```

### 1b — AWS Backup

AWS Backup emits CloudWatch Events whenever a backup job changes state. Forward via the AWS TA. Path: Splunk_TA_aws → CloudWatch Logs input → log group `/aws/backup/<region>`. In `props.conf`:
```
[aws:cloudwatch:backup_jobs]
sourcetype = backup:job_status
EVAL-workload_id = mvfilter(host, resource_arn)
EVAL-status = case(state="COMPLETED","success", state="FAILED","failed", state="PARTIAL","partial", true(),"unknown")
```

References: AWS Backup CloudWatch Events documentation linked under References. Note that AWS Backup events identify the workload by `resourceArn` — convert to your canonical `workload_id` via a regex/lookup at parse time so the join in the SPL works.

### 1c — Azure Backup

Azure Recovery Services Vaults emit diagnostic logs with `category=AzureBackupReport`. Configure diagnostic settings on every Recovery Services Vault: forward `AzureBackupReport` to Event Hub, ingest via the Microsoft Cloud Services TA. Set sourcetype to `backup:job_status`. The Azure schema names the workload via `BackupItemUniqueId` — alias to `workload_id` in `props.conf`.

### 1d — Regulated-workload register

The daily CMDB dump runs as a scheduled report on the ServiceNow side OR as a Splunk scheduled job using `| servicenow` (from TA 1928). Recommended pattern: a daily Splunk scheduled search
```spl
| servicenow table=cmdb_ci_server query="regulated_class!=NULL^decommissioned=false"
| table name regulated_class tier protection_window_hours cmdb_ci business_owner provisioned_at decommissioned_at protection_method
| rename name AS workload_id
| outputlookup regulated_workloads.csv
```
schedule cron `0 3 * * *`. Pair with a freshness alert (see Prerequisites).

## Step 2 — Implement the search and alert

Walkthrough of the production SPL:

1. `| inputlookup regulated_workloads.csv` — the inverted-Splunk pattern: the search starts from the AUTHORITATIVE register, not from events. The register is the source of truth for what should be backed up; events tell us whether it was.
2. `| where isnull(decommissioned_at) OR strptime(decommissioned_at,"%Y-%m-%d") > now()` — decommissioned workloads exit scope at their decommissioning timestamp. Decommissioning a workload is itself a SOX-relevant operation and should be evidenced in the change-record (out of scope here).
3. `eval protection_window_hours=coalesce(protection_window_hours, case(...))` — explicit value preferred; tier defaults as fallback so that workloads with a missing column don't silently use 0.
4. `join type=left workload_id [...]` — the join with backup events. `type=left` is critical — workloads with NO matching backup event still appear with `last_success_at=null`, and that's the `no_backup_record_found` finding. Without `type=left` they'd disappear silently.
5. Inside the join sub-search: `earliest=-14d` deliberately exceeds the longest tier-3 protection window (168h = 7d) by 2× — this guards against late-arriving forwarded events (which we've seen for offshore tape vaults). `where job_status=="success"` enforces that only genuinely successful jobs count as coverage (a 'failed' or 'partial' event is worse than nothing — it represents an active gap).
6. The CRITICAL DEFAULT — Splunk's `join` sub-search limit defaults to 50,000 result rows. If your environment has >50k regulated workloads, the join silently truncates. Either raise `maxresultrows` in `limits.conf` (NOT recommended without operational pre-review) OR refactor to:
   ```spl
   <stream A> | stats max(_time) as last_success_at by workload_id
   | append [<stream B / lookup>]
   | stats <reduce> by workload_id
   ```
   which is bigger to read but unbounded. The given SPL is correct for ≤50k workloads — most enterprises.
7. `has_successful_backup_in_window` is the central boolean. `where ...==0` keeps only the gaps.
8. The `finding` field distinguishes the two operational classes; the runbook in Step 4 handles each differently.

### Schedule

```ini
[UC-22.45.3: Regulated workloads with backup coverage gap]
disabled = 0
enableSched = 1
cron_schedule = 0 4 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -14d
dispatch.latest_time = now
alert.suppress = 1
alert.suppress.period = 23h
alert.suppress.fields = workload_id
action.summary_index = 1
action.summary_index._name = audit_evidence
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.45.3,reg=NIST_800-53,reg=DORA,reg=SOX,reg=PCI
action.servicenow_grc = 1
action.servicenow_grc.category = BCP-COVERAGE-GAP
```

Daily cadence at 04:00 is intentional: most backup engines complete the previous evening's runs by 03:00 site-local, and most CMDBs have already refreshed by 03:30. Running before either is finished causes spurious findings.

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

### (a) End-to-end cross-check on a known-good workload

Pick a workload you know was backed up overnight. Confirm in the backup engine UI (Veeam Backup Console / AWS Backup console / Azure Recovery Services / Commvault Command Center) that the job succeeded.

In Splunk:
```spl
index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status workload_id="<that-workload>" earliest=-1d
| stats latest(_time) as last_evt, latest(status) as last_status by workload_id
| eval last_evt_iso=strftime(last_evt, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z")
```
The `last_evt_iso` must match the UI's reported completion time to within the ingestion latency (typically <15 minutes). If empty, drill into `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ExecProcessor backup_job_status` for the modular-input run history.

### (b) Register sanity check

Does the register actually contain regulated workloads? Sounds trivial; it isn't.
```spl
| inputlookup regulated_workloads.csv
| stats count by regulated_class
```
Expect non-zero counts for every class you operate in. A zero count means the CMDB feed is filtering out the class — usually a category-name mismatch (`SOX` vs `Sarbanes` vs `S-Ox`).

### (c) Inverse check — workloads in events but missing from register

A workload that appears in backup events but NOT in the regulated-workload register may be unregistered scope leakage (someone backed up a PCI-scope system without registering it) OR an out-of-scope workload that happens to be backed up (fine, but worth knowing). Sister-search:
```spl
index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status status=success earliest=-7d
| stats max(_time) as last_success_at by workload_id
| lookup regulated_workloads.csv workload_id OUTPUT regulated_class
| where isnull(regulated_class)
```
Review weekly with the data-protection team — every entry needs a decision (register or document the out-of-scope rationale).

### (d) Fixture replay

Load `sample-data/uc-22.45.3-fixture.json`. It contains a known-good workload (must NOT appear), a stale-coverage workload (must appear as `no_backup_in_window`), a new workload with no events (must appear as `no_backup_record_found`), a decommissioned-yesterday workload (must NOT appear), a workload with only failed events in the window (must appear), and an array-snapshot-protected workload (must NOT appear once `protection_method=array_snapshot` filter is added). Expected output: three rows.

### (e) Per-class attestation

```spl
| inputlookup regulated_workloads.csv
| where isnull(decommissioned_at)
| join type=left workload_id [ search index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status status=success earliest=-14d | stats max(_time) as last_success_at by workload_id ]
| eval covered=if(isnotnull(last_success_at) AND last_success_at >= now() - coalesce(protection_window_hours,168)*3600, 1, 0)
| stats sum(covered) as covered, count as total by regulated_class
| eval coverage_pct=round(100*covered/total, 2)
```
This is the steering-pack number. Treat anything below 100% on PCI/HIPAA/SOX as a P1 incident; anything below 99.5% on GDPR as a P2.

## Step 4 — Operationalise

### Dashboard `UC-22.45.3 — Backup Coverage`

Panels:
1. **Row 1 — class tiles.** Four single-value panels, one per regulated_class, each showing the coverage % with red/amber/green thresholds appropriate to the class.
2. **Row 2 — finding table** from the production SPL with drilldown.
3. **Row 3 — 13-week heatmap of gaps by class × week.** Trend should be flat-low or descending.
4. **Row 4 — per-business-owner table** showing open gaps. The steering pack reads this row to assign accountability — open gaps without an assigned owner is the second-worst pattern.
5. **Row 5 — top-N CI types** contributing gaps. Surfaces design-level gaps (e.g. 'all our managed-Kubernetes workloads are missing backups — we never engineered backup for them').

### Runbook

**On `finding=no_backup_record_found`:**
1. Check `protection_method` — is this an array-snapshot or DB-native workload that shouldn't be in this UC?
2. If `protection_method=agent` or null, confirm with the backup-engine team whether a job exists. If yes, the join failed on a `workload_id` mismatch — fix at the CMDB or the agent registration.
3. If no job exists, open a `BCP-COVERAGE-GAP` ticket; SLA: protected within 24h for tier-1, 72h for tier-2/3.

**On `finding=no_backup_in_window`:**
1. Cross-reference UC-22.45.1 — is the workload also a restore-test miss? If yes, the workload is in serious recovery jeopardy — escalate.
2. Check `index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status workload_id="<x>" earliest=-30d | timechart span=1d count by status` — sudden cessation vs gradual degradation vs always-failing.
3. If always-failing, the backup config is broken for this workload — engage the backup-engine vendor.
4. If recent cessation, correlate with change events: `index=changes earliest=-7d workload_id="<x>"`.

### Evidence pack (weekly)

A scheduled report runs the per-class attestation query, exports a one-page PDF with the coverage-% scoreboard plus the full list of open gaps, RFC3161-signs it, and uploads to `evidence-packs/cp-9/<year>/<isoweek>/UC-22.45.3.{pdf,csv,tsr}`. The SOX-tagged subset goes into the SOX evidence binder separately.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

### First-line triage when the result set is wrong

Walk these checks in order before assuming the SPL is broken:

1. **No backup events for a regulated workload over 24h** — `index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status workload_id=<x> earliest=-1d | stats count`. Zero means the workload truly has no recent backup OR the TA / HEC poller is broken. Drill into `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ExecProcessor "veeam" OR "powerprotect" OR "rubrik"` for the modular input run history.
2. **TA credential expired** — Veeam EM credentials, ServiceNow CMDB credentials, AWS IAM roles, and Azure service-principal secrets all rotate on different schedules. A rotation that wasn't reflected in the TA setup surfaces as a `permission denied` / `403` / `401` error in `index=_internal component=ExecProcessor`. Cross-check `| rest /services/admin/passwords | search realm=*aws* OR realm=*veeam*` for the last credential update time.
3. **NULL values in the canonical join field** — `index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status earliest=-1d | where isnull(workload_id) | stats count`. Non-zero means the parse-time normalization (coalesce of host/vm_name/client_name/resource_id) is failing for at least one vendor — usually a TA upgrade that renamed a field.
4. **Modular input timeout on AWS Backup CloudWatch ingestion** — the AWS TA's CloudWatch Logs subscription has a 1.5MB/s per shard limit; spike days exceed it and the input falls behind. Surface with `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd "aws_cloudwatch_logs" "timeout" OR "throttl"`.
5. **Lookup row count anomaly** — a CMDB export that dumped fewer records than the previous day suggests a query-filter regression upstream. Sister-alert on >20% row-count drop.

### Common failure modes

- **CMDB feed stale, false-flagging decommissioned workloads.** The 24-hour freshness alert catches this. Fix the feed before tuning the SPL — silent decommissioned workloads are evidence the inventory itself is broken.
- **Workload-id mismatch between CMDB (`SAP-PRD-01`) and backup engine (`sap-prd-01.example.com`).** Normalise at INGEST: `props.conf` for `backup:job_status` should EVAL `workload_id` to a canonical form (lowercased short-name, no domain). Document the convention in `regulated_workloads.csv` headers.
- **Join silently truncated >50k workloads.** Check the search job inspector — if `result_count` exits the sub-search at exactly 50,000, you're hitting `maxresultrows`. Refactor to `stats` + `append` as described in Step 2.
- **Cloud workloads missing.** AWS Backup CloudWatch Events route to your AWS account's regional log group — ensure the Splunk TA is subscribed to EVERY region in scope, not just `us-east-1`. Similarly for Azure: every Recovery Services Vault in every subscription needs diagnostic settings.
- **Bytes_protected = 0 on every success** — this can hide silent corruption (the job 'succeeded' but protected nothing). Add a sister-search alert: `index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status status=success bytes_protected=0 earliest=-1d | stats count by workload_id` and treat hits as `partial` for the purposes of this UC.

### SOX-specific caveats

- **Material weakness threshold.** An ITGC operating ineffectively for a 'financially significant' workload is a control deficiency that can rise to a material weakness if not remediated promptly. Tier-1 SOX workloads showing as gaps for >5 business days require an immediate escalation to the SOX programme manager — do not let them stack to the next quarter.
- **Auditor sampling.** External auditors will sample N workloads from your SOX scope and ask for backup proof. Your evidence is the daily attestation; have it ready in a way that lets the auditor trace from one of their sampled workload_ids to the daily attestation row to the source backup-job event.

### PCI DSS v4.0-specific caveats

- **Scope determination (Req. 12.5).** Misclassified scope is the #1 PCI finding. Run the inverse check (3c) weekly and review every workload flagged 'in backup events, not in PCI register'. Some will legitimately be non-PCI; others will be quiet PCI scope creep. Decide each explicitly.
- **9.4.3 secure storage.** This UC enforces coverage; UC-22.45.2 (immutability) and UC-22.45.4 (TLS) enforce secure storage. Tie all three together in the PCI evidence binder under Requirement 9.

### DORA-specific caveats

- **Critical-and-important-function scope.** DORA Art.12 applies to ICT systems supporting critical and important functions, which is narrower than 'all systems'. Make sure the `regulated_workloads.csv` register reflects the DORA scoping decision documented in the management body's Information & ICT Risk Management Framework — not just what your CMDB happens to tag.
- **Article 27 record retention.** Coverage records — the daily attestations — must be retained 5+ years. The 7-year `audit_evidence` retention satisfies this.
grandmaExplanation: We compare the list of important systems against the list of systems that actually got backed up this week. If something on the important list is missing from the backup list, you see it before the auditor — and before the next outage forces you to find out.
